FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (release 5.1), N=4,754 youth with baseline and year 4 data

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables: video game hours, social media hours, smartphones; also problematic screen use via VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire)

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports independent linear mixed-effects regression models testing whether baseline individual differences (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognitive ability) predict ST and PSU outcomes at ages 12-15

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 & 4 report β, SE, p-values for associations (e.g., video game hours: Urgency b=.07, SE=0.01, p<.001; social media hours: Urgency b=.06, SE=0.01, p<.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; prospective design predicting screen time and problematic screen use at ages 12-15 from baseline measures at ages 9-10

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 applies: (a) Smartphone/SM variables (ST, PSU of video games, social media, smartphones) are the DEPENDENT variables in all reported analyses; (b) ALL predictors are individual psychological/behavioral traits (urgency, reward sensitivity, punishment sensitivity, externalizing, internalizing, cognitive ability) plus demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity, income, parental education); (c) ALL analyses are LONGITUDINAL/PROSPECTIVE (baseline ages 9-10 predicting year 4 outcomes ages 12-15). No cross-sectional analyses, no SM as IV with non-SM outcome, no mediation/moderation involving SM.
notes: Paper reports separate analyses by screen activity (video games, social media) and outcome type (ST vs PSU). Screen time and PSU only modestly correlated (r=0.27-0.28), supporting distinct constructs. Study qualifies as primary inclusion target for FT-EC7 exclusion criterion review.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, baseline N=4,042 (from ABCD data release 2.0.1 with 11,875 participants at baseline)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity is listed as a KEY PREDICTIVE VARIABLE in the abstract/results but is NOT the exposure/outcome under primary investigation. The PRIMARY outcome is Delayed Reward Discounting (DRD), a cognitive decision-making measure. Screen time serves as a COVARIATE/PREDICTOR in machine learning models predicting DRD. Fail: smartphone/SM is neither IV nor DV in the hypothesized association of interest.

FT_IC3.evidence: Not applicable: FT_IC2 not met. No inferential test specifically linking smartphone/SM variable to a non-SM outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: Not applicable: FT_IC2 not met. Paper does not report extractable coefficients for SM↔outcome associations; screen media only mentioned as contributing feature in ML feature importance, without specific numerical values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; prospective machine learning study predicting one-year-follow-up DRD from baseline measures

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 not applicable: EXCLUDED at FT_IC2 criterion. Screen media is neither the primary exposure nor the primary outcome; it appears only as one predictor among many in ML models predicting DRD (a cognitive outcome, not phone/SM use).
notes: This is a machine learning study where screen time is one among many behavioral/demographic predictors. The primary causal direction tested is: baseline characteristics (including screen time) → future DRD (not: screen time → behavioral outcome).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, N=10,650 children (5,112 girls, 5,538 boys) aged 9.9±0.6 years, cross-sectional baseline assessment

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen media variables: TV watching, video watching (YouTube), online games, social media (Twitter, Instagram), mature-rated video games, mature-rated movies. Operationalized via 7-point Likert scale for daily usage hours.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports multilevel general linear models (for continuous screen time in minutes/day) and multilevel logistic regression models (for meeting vs not meeting ≤2h/day recommendation). Tests associations between parental psychological problems (predictor) and children's screen time/media behaviors (outcomes).

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports OR, 95% CI, and regression coefficients: 'Parental internalising problems were associated with children watching mature-rated movies (OR=1.14, 95% CI=1.00, 1.30) and playing mature-rated games (OR=1.27, 95% CI=1.11, 1.45)'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD baseline data examining associations between parental psychological problems and children's screen media behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Acta Paediatrica, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does NOT apply. Fails criterion (c): Study is CROSS-SECTIONAL (not longitudinal/prospective). Additionally, fails criterion (b): Parental psychological problems include psychopathology/clinical dimensions (internalizing, externalizing tendencies), not ONLY pure demographics (SES, race, sex, age, etc.). Per FT-EC7 rule, includes individual psychological/clinical predictor → EC7 does not apply; keep INCLUDE.
notes: Study examines parenting/family-level predictors of child screen time. Cross-sectional design and inclusion of parental psychopathology (not just demographics) mean FT-EC7 criterion does not apply. Paper remains included.
